1.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 1
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God calls Judah to return to Him (unlike their fathers who ignored the prophets), and Zechariah sees a night vision of horsemen patrolling the earth — the nations are at ease while Jerusalem remains unrestored, prompting God's jealous compassion.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Vision — The Call to Return and the
Horsemen Among the Myrtles
Key Themes: A call to repentance, God's jealousy for Jerusalem,
angelic patrols reporting on the earth, God's comfort and
restoration, the four horns and four craftsmen

Historical Context: Zechariah begins his ministry in the eighth
month of the second year of Darius (October/November 520 BC) —
two months after Haggai's first oracle. Like Haggai, Zechariah
encourages the returned exiles to rebuild the Temple, but his
approach is vastly different: where Haggai is direct and
practical, Zechariah is visionary and apocalyptic.

The opening call (vv.1-6) establishes the book's foundation:
"Return to me, and I will return to you." God reminds this
generation not to repeat their fathers' mistakes. The fathers
ignored the prophets and suffered exile. But God's word outlasted
them all — it "overtook" them (v.6). The lesson: human life is
short; God's word endures forever.

The first night vision (vv.7-17) reveals horsemen patrolling the
earth and reporting peace. But the peace of the nations contrasts
with Jerusalem's suffering. The Angel of the LORD intercedes:
"How long?" God responds with "good and comforting words" —
He is jealous for Zion and angry with the complacent nations.
The second vision (vv.18-21) shows four horns (empires that
scattered Israel) matched by four craftsmen who will cast them
down.

Structure:
- The Call to Repentance: "Return to Me" (vv.1-6)
- The First Night Vision: Horsemen Among the Myrtles (vv.7-11)
- The Angel's Intercession and God's Answer (vv.12-17)
- The Second Vision: Four Horns and Four Craftsmen (vv.18-21)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.3              | James       | 4       | 8                  | "Draw near to God, and he will draw near to you." (ESV) — The NT echo of Zechariah's call to return
v.3              | Malachi     | 3       | 7                  | "Return to me, and I will return to you, says the LORD of hosts." (ESV) — the same invitation repeated by the last prophet
v.5              | Isaiah      | 40      | 6-8                | "The grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our God will stand forever." (ESV)
v.8              | Revelation  | 6       | 1-8                | The four horsemen of Revelation echo Zechariah's colored horses patrolling the earth
v.12             | Daniel      | 9       | 2                  | Daniel also understood the seventy years from Jeremiah and prayed for restoration
v.14             | Exodus      | 20      | 5                  | "I the LORD your God am a jealous God." (ESV) — God's jealousy is a covenant attribute
v.16             | Haggai      | 1       | 8                  | "Go up to the hills and bring wood and build the house." (ESV) — the same command through Zechariah's contemporary
v.21             | Daniel      | 2       | 44                 | God raises up kingdoms to cast down the empires that oppose His people

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "the son of Berechiah, the son of Iddo" — Zechariah was grandson of Iddo (cf. Ezra 5:1, 6:14 where he is called "son of Iddo" — "son" can mean descendant). Berechiah means "the LORD blesses"; Iddo means "appointed time." His very lineage declares God's blessing at the appointed time.
v.8              | KJV         | "speckled" (KJV) / "sorrel" (ESV) — Hebrew "seruqqim" = reddish-brown or spotted. The different colored horses may represent different divine missions or judgments, as in Revelation's four horsemen.
v.12             | KJV         | "threescore and ten years" = 70 years from the Temple's destruction in 586 BC to approximately 516 BC (its completion). Or from the first deportation (605 BC) to the return decree (538 BC). Either way, the time is fulfilled.
v.21             | KJV         | "carpenters" (KJV) / "craftsmen" (ESV) — Hebrew "charashim" = skilled workers, artisans. God raises up agents of judgment against the empires (horns) that scattered His people. For every horn, God has a craftsman.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Return to me, and I will return to you" (v.3). God does not say "Clean
   up first, then come." He says: take one step toward Me, and I will come
   running toward you. The initiative is His; the response is yours. Have
   you drifted? The way back is simpler than you think.

2. "Your fathers, where are they?" (v.5). One generation's disobedience does
   not have to define the next. God gives this generation a fresh start. You
   are not bound by your parents' failures — you can choose differently. But
   you must choose.

3. God is "jealous for Zion" (v.14). His jealousy means He will not give up
   on you. He will not share you with lesser loves. He pursues with fierce,
   protective affection. That jealousy is not a flaw — it is the intensity
   of His commitment.

4. For every "horn" there is a "craftsman" (vv.18-21). Whatever power has
   scattered, broken, or opposed you — God has already prepared the agent of
   its overthrow. The horns seem powerful, but the craftsmen are coming. God
   always has the last move.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Zechariah Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/zechariah/

David Guzik — Zechariah 1 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/zechariah-1/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

1. And I lifted my eyes and saw, and behold, a man with a measuring line in his hand!

3. And behold, the angel who talked with me came forward, and another angel came forward to meet him
4. and said to him, “Run, say to that young man, ‘Jerusalem shall be inhabited as villages without walls, because of the multitude of people and livestock in it.
5. And I will be to her a wall of fire all around, declares the LORD, and I will be the glory in her midst.’”

7. Up! Escape to Zion, you who dwell with the daughter of Babylon.
8. For thus said the LORD of hosts, after his glory sent me to the nations who plundered you, for he who touches you touches the apple of his eye:
9. “Behold, I will shake my hand over them, and they shall become plunder for those who served them. Then you will know that the LORD of hosts has sent me.
10. Sing and rejoice, O daughter of Zion, for behold, I come and I will dwell in your midst, declares the LORD.
11. And many nations shall join themselves to the LORD in that day, and shall be my people. And I will dwell in your midst, and you shall know that the LORD of hosts has sent me to you.
12. And the LORD will inherit Judah as his portion in the holy land, and will again choose Jerusalem.”
13. Be silent, all flesh, before the LORD, for he has roused himself from his holy dwelling.

5. For I,’ says Yahweh, ‘will be to her a wall of fire around it, and I will be the glory in the middle of her.
6. Come! Come! Flee from the land of the north,’ says Yahweh; ‘for I have spread you abroad as the four winds of the sky,’ says Yahweh.
7. ‘Come, Zion! Escape, you who dwell with the daughter of Babylon.’
8. For Yahweh of Armies says: ‘For honor he has sent me to the nations which plundered you; for he who touches you touches the apple of his eye.
9. For, behold, I will shake my hand over them, and they will be a plunder to those who served them; and you will know that Yahweh of Armies has sent me.
10. Sing and rejoice, daughter of Zion; for, behold, I come, and I will dwell within you,’ says Yahweh.
11. Many nations shall join themselves to Yahweh in that day, and shall be my people; and I will dwell among you, and you shall know that Yahweh of Armies has sent me to you.
12. Yahweh will inherit Judah as his portion in the holy land, and will again choose Jerusalem.
13. Be silent, all flesh, before Yahweh; for he has roused himself from his holy habitation!”
2.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 2
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Zechariah sees a man measuring Jerusalem and is told the city will be inhabited without walls because of its multitude — God Himself will be a wall of fire around her and the glory within.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Vision — The Man with the Measuring
Line and God as a Wall of Fire
Key Themes: The future expansion of Jerusalem, God as
supernatural protection, the call to flee Babylon, the nations
joining the LORD, God dwelling in Zion's midst

Historical Context: This third night vision (the measuring line)
addresses Jerusalem's future with extravagant promise. A young
man sets out to measure Jerusalem — presumably to plan its walls.
But an angel stops him: Jerusalem will be so prosperous and
populated that no wall can contain it. Instead of man-made walls,
God Himself will be "a wall of fire" around her.

The vision speaks to the returned exiles' anxiety. They were a
tiny remnant in a ruined city surrounded by hostile neighbors.
Walls meant safety. But God says: I AM your wall. My presence
is your protection. And the city will outgrow any boundary you
could build. This is eschatological — pointing to the final
Messianic Jerusalem that cannot be contained.

The call to "flee from the land of the north" (vv.6-7) urges
Jews still in Babylon to return. Many had grown comfortable in
exile. But God's plan centers on Jerusalem, and His people
belong there. The chapter climaxes with the magnificent promise:
"I come, and I will dwell in the midst of thee" (v.10).

Structure:
- The Third Vision: A Man Measuring Jerusalem (vv.1-2)
- The Angel's Message: No Walls Needed (vv.3-5)
- The Call to Flee Babylon (vv.6-7)
- The Apple of God's Eye (v.8)
- The LORD's Coming to Dwell in Zion (vv.10-12)
- Universal Silence Before the LORD (v.13)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.4              | Isaiah      | 54      | 2-3                | "Enlarge the place of your tent... for you will spread abroad to the right and to the left." (ESV)
v.5              | Exodus      | 13      | 21                 | The pillar of fire that led and protected Israel in the wilderness—God as fire-wall
v.5              | Isaiah      | 60      | 19                 | "The LORD will be your everlasting light, and your God will be your glory." (ESV)
v.8              | Deuteronomy | 32      | 10                 | "He kept him as the apple of his eye." (ESV) — God's tender care for Israel
v.10             | John        | 1       | 14                 | "And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us." (ESV) — the ultimate fulfillment of God dwelling in Zion
v.11             | Ephesians   | 2       | 13-14              | Gentiles brought near through Christ, making one people from Jew and Gentile
v.13             | Habakkuk    | 2       | 20                 | "But the LORD is in his holy temple; let all the earth keep silence before him." (ESV)
v.13             | Zephaniah   | 1       | 7                  | "Be silent before the Lord GOD!" (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.4              | KJV         | "towns without walls" (KJV) / "villages without walls" (ESV) — Hebrew "perazot" = open, unwalled settlements. The picture is of a city so secure it needs no fortification — because God IS the fortification.
v.5              | KJV         | "wall of fire" — Not a static barrier but a CONSUMING fire that destroys any who approach with hostile intent. God's protection is active, not passive.
v.8              | KJV         | "apple of his eye" — Hebrew "babat eino" = the pupil, the gate of the eye. The most delicate, reflexively-protected part of the body. God protects Israel as instinctively as you protect your own eye.
v.13             | KJV         | "raised up" (KJV) / "roused himself" (ESV) — Hebrew "ne'or" = to be awakened, stirred up. As if God has been silent and now rises from His dwelling to act decisively. When God "rouses Himself," history changes.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God's plan is always bigger than our measurements (vv.1-4). The young man
   tries to measure Jerusalem — to define its limits. But God's vision for
   your life exceeds what you can measure or plan. Stop trying to contain
   what God is building. His blessing will overflow your boundaries.

2. "A wall of fire" (v.5). Your best defense is not human strategy but
   divine presence. When God surrounds you with fire, no enemy can
   penetrate. Stop building walls of anxiety and self-protection. Let God be
   your wall.

3. You are the "apple of His eye" (v.8). God is not indifferent to what
   touches you. Every attack against you touches HIM in His most sensitive
   place. You are that precious to Him. Live with the confidence of someone
   fiercely loved and protected.

4. "I come, and I will dwell in the midst of thee" (v.10). God's ultimate
   goal is not just to bless FROM a distance but to dwell WITH you. Emmanuel
   — God with us. The Christian life is not about getting things from God;
   it is about having GOD Himself.

5. "Be silent, all flesh" (v.13). Sometimes the holiest response is silence.
   When God is working, stop talking. Stop planning. Stop worrying. Be still
   and let God be God.

3.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 3
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Joshua the high priest stands accused by Satan but is cleansed by the Angel of the LORD, given pure garments, and told of the coming "Branch" — God will remove the iniquity of the land in a single day.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Vision — The Cleansing of Joshua the
High Priest and the Promise of the Branch
Key Themes: Satan as accuser, divine justification, filthy
garments removed, clean garments bestowed, the Branch promised,
iniquity removed in one day, the gospel in the Old Testament

Historical Context: The fourth night vision is arguably the most
theologically profound in Zechariah. Joshua the high priest
stands before the Angel of the LORD — representing Israel before
God. Satan stands at his right hand to accuse him. And the
accusation is valid: Joshua wears FILTHY garments (literally
"excrement-covered" in Hebrew). He is guilty.

But God does not defend Joshua's innocence — He overrules the
accusation by sovereign grace. "Is not this a brand plucked from
the fire?" God removes the filthy garments, clothes Joshua in
clean vestments, and places a clean turban on his head. This is
justification: not making the sinner righteous in himself, but
CLOTHING him with a righteousness not his own.

The chapter then pivots to the ultimate resolution: "I will bring
forth my servant the BRANCH" (v.8). The Branch is the Messiah —
the one who will "remove the iniquity of that land in one day"
(v.9). This is the cross. One day. One sacrifice. All sin removed.

Structure:
- Joshua Before the Angel: Satan the Accuser (vv.1-2)
- The Filthy Garments Removed (vv.3-4)
- The Clean Turban and Pure Vestments (v.5)
- The Charge to Walk Faithfully (vv.6-7)
- The Promise of the Branch (v.8)
- The Stone with Seven Eyes: Iniquity Removed in One Day (v.9)
- The Millennial Peace: Vine and Fig Tree (v.10)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Job         | 1       | 6-12               | Satan appearing before God to accuse—the same pattern as in Job
v.1              | Revelation  | 12      | 10                 | Satan as "the accuser of our brothers" (ESV) cast down
v.2              | ?           | ?       | ?                  | Jude 9 — "The Lord rebuke you" (ESV) — Michael uses the same words contending with the devil.
v.4              | Isaiah      | 61      | 10                 | "He has clothed me with the garments of salvation; he has covered me with the robe of righteousness." (ESV)
v.4              | 2 Corinthians | 5       | 21                 | "For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God." (ESV)
v.8              | Isaiah      | 11      | 1                  | "There shall come forth a shoot from the stump of Jesse, and a branch from his roots shall bear fruit." (ESV)
v.8              | Jeremiah    | 23      | 5                  | "I will raise up for David a righteous Branch." (ESV)
v.9              | Hebrews     | 10      | 12                 | "But when Christ had offered for all time a single sacrifice for sins, he sat down at the right hand of God." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "filthy garments" — Hebrew "tso'im" = excrement-stained. This is not mere dirtiness — it is the most revolting defilement possible. Isaiah uses the same root in 64:6: "all our righteousnesses are as filthy rags." Our best efforts are sewage before God's holiness.
v.4              | KJV         | "change of raiment" (KJV) / "pure vestments" (ESV) — Hebrew "machalatsot" = festival/ceremonial garments. These are not work clothes but CELEBRATION garments — the finest attire for the most honored occasion.
v.5              | KJV         | "fair mitre" (KJV) / "clean turban" (ESV) — Hebrew "tsanif tahor" = a pure/clean turban. The high priest's turban bore the inscription "HOLINESS TO THE LORD" (Exodus 28:36). Joshua receives back his priestly authority — now purified.
v.8              | KJV         | "the BRANCH" — Hebrew "tsemach" = a sprout, a growing shoot. It emphasizes organic growth from seemingly dead stock. The Messiah grows up from David's cut-down tree — humble beginnings, glorious destiny.
v.9              | KJV         | "seven eyes" — Representing omniscience and perfect watchfulness. God sees completely. The stone (likely the cornerstone of the Temple, or Messiah Himself) is under God's perfect, all-seeing care.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Satan accuses, but God defends (vv.1-2). The enemy's strategy is
   accusation — reminding you of your guilt, your failures, your
   unworthiness. But God says: "I rebuke you." Your defender is stronger
   than your accuser. When condemnation rises, appeal to the Judge who
   already ruled in your favor.

2. Your filth is not the final word (vv.3-4). Joshua stood in
   excrement-stained garments — and was NOT rejected. God removed the filth
   and clothed him in purity. No matter how stained you feel, God's grace is
   greater than your sin. He does not just forgive — He RE-CLOTHES.

3. Justification is God's work, not yours (v.4). Joshua did not clean
   himself. He did not earn the new garments. He simply STOOD THERE while
   God did the work. Salvation is received, not achieved. Stop trying to
   wash your own filthy garments. Let God dress you in His righteousness.

4. "In one day" (v.9). The complete removal of sin happened in ONE day — the
   day Christ died. Not a process but an event. Not gradual improvement but
   decisive atonement. If you are in Christ, your iniquity was removed —
   completely, finally, irrevocably — in one day on Calvary.

5. The Branch grows from lowly places (v.8). The Messiah comes not as a
   mighty oak but as a small shoot. God's greatest work often begins in the
   smallest, humblest circumstances. Don't despise your small beginnings —
   the Branch is growing.

4.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 4
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The vision of the golden lampstand and two olive trees teaches that the temple will be rebuilt "not by might nor by power, but by My Spirit" — Zerubbabel's hands laid the foundation and will complete it.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Vision — The Golden Lampstand, the
Two Olive Trees, and the Spirit's Power
Key Themes: The sufficiency of the Spirit, human weakness and
divine power, the day of small things, Zerubbabel's
completion of the Temple, continuous supply of God's anointing

Historical Context: The fifth night vision reveals a golden
lampstand (menorah) with a bowl on top and seven lamps — each
with seven channels (49 total openings for light). Flanking it
are two olive trees that continuously feed oil into the bowl.
The lamp never goes out because the supply never ceases.

The interpretation centers on Zerubbabel's task of rebuilding the
Temple. The mountain of opposition seems insurmountable. But God
declares one of the most famous verses in Scripture: "Not by
might, nor by power, but by my spirit, saith the LORD of hosts."
Human resources cannot complete God's work. Only the Spirit can.
The mountain will become a plain before Zerubbabel.

The "day of small things" (v.10) directly addresses the
discouragement of the remnant. The work seems insignificant. The
Temple seems modest. But God delights in small beginnings
empowered by His Spirit. The two olive trees (v.14) are "the
two anointed ones" — Joshua and Zerubbabel (priest and king) —
who stand before the Lord, continually supplied with His oil.

Structure:
- The Vision: A Golden Lampstand with Olive Trees (vv.1-3)
- Zechariah's Question and the Angel's Challenge (vv.4-5)
- "Not by Might... But by My Spirit" (v.6)
- The Mountain Becomes a Plain (v.7)
- Zerubbabel Will Finish the Temple (vv.8-9)
- The Day of Small Things (v.10)
- The Two Olive Trees Explained (vv.11-14)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Exodus      | 25      | 31-37              | The original Tabernacle lampstand (menorah) — the pattern for Zechariah's vision
v.6              | 2 Corinthians | 12      | 9                  | "My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness." (ESV)
v.6              | 1 Samuel    | 17      | 47                 | "The battle is the LORD's" (ESV) — David's declaration against Goliath echoes this principle
v.7              | Isaiah      | 40      | 4                  | "Every mountain and hill shall be made low" (ESV) — the same leveling imagery
v.7              | Matthew     | 17      | 20                 | "If you have faith... you will say to this mountain, 'Move from here to there,' and it will move." (ESV)
v.9              | Philippians | 1       | 6                  | "He who began a good work in you will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ." (ESV)
v.10             | 1 Corinthians | 1       | 27-28              | God chooses the weak and small things to shame the mighty
v.14             | Revelation  | 11      | 3-4                | The two witnesses described as "two olive trees... standing before the Lord of the earth." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "candlestick" (KJV) / "lampstand" (ESV) — Hebrew "menorah." Not a candle but an oil lamp. The distinction matters: oil is consistently a symbol of the Holy Spirit in Scripture. The lamp burns by OIL, not wax — by Spirit, not human fuel.
v.6              | KJV         | "might" — Hebrew "chayil" = force, army, wealth, resources, efficiency. "Power" — Hebrew "koach" = individual strength, vigor, capacity. Together they cover every human resource — collective and individual. NEITHER suffices for God's work.
v.7              | KJV         | "headstone" (KJV) / "top stone" (ESV) — Hebrew "ha'eben harosha" = the head/chief stone. The capstone or cornerstone that completes the building. Its placement amid shouts of "Grace!" means the entire project — start to finish — is credited to God's grace, not human achievement.
v.10             | KJV         | "plummet" (KJV) / "plumb line" (ESV) — Hebrew "ha'eben habedil" = the tin/lead stone used to ensure straight construction. Zerubbabel holds the measuring tool — he is actively building. The work is real, but the power is God's.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit" (v.6). This is the death
   sentence to self-reliance. Your talent, your resources, your strategic
   plans — none of them accomplish God's purposes. Only His Spirit does. The
   question is not "How much can I do?" but "How much of the Spirit am I
   depending on?"

2. Your "great mountain" will become a plain (v.7). That insurmountable
   obstacle — the debt, the addiction, the broken relationship, the
   impossible task — is addressed to directly: "Who are YOU, O great
   mountain?" Before God's anointed servant, mountains flatten. Speak to
   your mountain in faith.

3. Don't despise the day of small things (v.10). If your ministry is small,
   your progress is slow, your resources are limited — do not despise it.
   God sees the plumb line in your hand. He rejoices over faithfulness in
   the small. Small + the Spirit = unstoppable.

4. The supply is continuous (vv.11-12). The olive trees feed oil CONSTANTLY
   into the lampstand. You don't need to stockpile anointing — you need to
   stay CONNECTED to the source. The Spirit flows continuously to those who
   remain positioned beside the Lord.

5. "Grace, grace!" (v.7). The shout at completion is not "Achievement!" or
   "Finally!" — it is "Grace!" Every finished work of God in your life will
   be attributed to His grace, not your grit. Live in grace. Finish in
   grace. Shout grace.

5.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 5
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Two visions — a flying scroll that curses every thief and perjurer, and a woman called Wickedness sealed in a basket and carried to Babylon — depict God's removal of sin from the land.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Vision — The Flying Scroll and the
Woman in the Basket
Key Themes: God's curse against sin, judgment on theft and
false oaths, the removal of wickedness from the land,
wickedness personified and exiled to Shinar (Babylon)

Historical Context: Visions six and seven deal with the
PURGING of sin from the land. If chapters 3-4 showed God's
grace (cleansing Joshua, empowering Zerubbabel), chapter 5
shows God's justice. Grace does not mean tolerance of sin.
The restored community must be holy.

The flying scroll (vv.1-4) is enormous — 20 cubits by 10 cubits
(30 feet by 15 feet) — the same dimensions as the porch of
Solomon's Temple (1 Kings 6:3). The curse written on it targets
two representative sins: stealing (against man) and swearing
falsely (against God). These cover both tables of the Law. The
curse enters the house and CONSUMES it — sin destroys from
within.

The woman in the basket (vv.5-11) personifies wickedness itself.
She is stuffed into an ephah (a measuring basket), sealed with a
lead cover, and carried by two winged women to the land of Shinar
(Babylon). The message: wickedness will be REMOVED from God's
land and sent back to where it belongs — Babylon, the biblical
symbol of human rebellion against God.

Structure:
- The Sixth Vision: The Flying Scroll (vv.1-4)
- The Scroll's Dimensions and Its Curse (vv.2-3)
- The Curse Entering the House of the Sinner (v.4)
- The Seventh Vision: The Woman in the Basket (vv.5-8)
- Wickedness Sealed Under Lead (vv.7-8)
- Wickedness Removed to Shinar (vv.9-11)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Ezekiel     | 2       | 9-10               | A scroll written on both sides with "lamentation and mourning and woe." (ESV)
v.3              | Deuteronomy | 27      | 15-26              | The covenant curses pronounced at Mount Ebal for disobedience
v.3              | Galatians   | 3       | 10                 | "Cursed be everyone who does not abide by all things written in the Book of the Law, and do them." (ESV)
v.4              | Malachi     | 3       | 5                  | God's curse against false swearers and those who defraud
v.8              | Revelation  | 17      | 3-5                | "Babylon the great, mother of prostitutes and of earth's abominations" (ESV) — wickedness personified and judged
v.11             | Genesis     | 11      | 1-9                | Shinar as the location of the Tower of Babel — the origin of organized rebellion against God
v.11             | Revelation  | 18      | 2                  | "Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great!" (ESV) — wickedness finally and fully judged

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "flying roll" (KJV) / "flying scroll" (ESV) — Hebrew "megillah 'aphah" = a scroll in flight. Scrolls in the ancient world contained legal documents and decrees. This flying scroll is a divine court order — God's verdict actively pursuing the guilty.
v.6              | KJV         | "ephah" — A dry measure of approximately one bushel (22 liters). It was the standard commercial measure — fitting that commercial wickedness (greed, theft) is contained in a commercial container.
v.7              | KJV         | "talent of lead" (KJV) / "leaden cover" (ESV) — Hebrew "kikkar ophereth" = a circle/disk of lead. Lead is the heaviest common metal — used to permanently seal wickedness shut. Once God confines sin, it cannot escape.
v.11             | KJV         | "Shinar" — The ancient name for Babylonia (Gen 10:10, 11:2). By sending wickedness to Shinar, God symbolically returns evil to its source. Babylon represents the world system opposed to God.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Sin has consequences that enter your "house" (v.4). The curse doesn't
   just visit — it REMAINS and CONSUMES. Hidden sin eats away at the
   structure of your life: your relationships, your peace, your integrity.
   Deal with it before it consumes the timber and stones of what you've
   built.

2. God takes theft and false speech equally seriously (v.3). One is against
   man, the other against God. Both merit the curse. Integrity in word and
   deed — this is what God requires. Guard both your hands AND your tongue.

3. God is actively REMOVING wickedness (vv.5-11). This is encouraging: God
   doesn't tolerate sin indefinitely. He is working to purge, contain, and
   exile it. In your life, He is working to remove wickedness — cooperate
   with the process. Don't cling to what God is trying to carry away.

4. Wickedness belongs in Babylon, not in Zion (v.11). Sin has no rightful
   place among God's people. When you tolerate sin in your life, you're
   keeping something in God's house that belongs in Shinar. Let God send it
   back where it came from.

6.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 6
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Four chariots go out to patrol the earth, and Zechariah is told to crown Joshua the high priest, symbolizing the coming "Branch" who will be both priest and king — building the true temple of the LORD.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Vision — The Four Chariots and the
Crowning of Joshua as the Branch
Key Themes: Divine sovereignty over the nations, the four
spirits/winds of heaven executing judgment, the Branch as
priest-king, the union of priestly and royal offices in one
Person, those "far off" joining in building God's temple

Historical Context: The eighth and final night vision shows
four chariots emerging from between two bronze mountains. These
are "the four spirits of the heavens" — divine agents of
judgment patrolling and pacifying the earth. The vision
bookends with vision one (the horsemen): in chapter 1, the
earth is at rest while Israel suffers; now God's agents bring
divine rest by executing judgment on the nations.

The crowning of Joshua (vv.9-15) is the theological climax of
the vision section. A PRIEST receives a CROWN — an act
unprecedented in Israel (priests wore turbans, kings wore
crowns). This symbolic act declares that the coming "Branch"
will unite the offices of priest and king in one Person. He
will "build the temple of the LORD" (the true Temple — Christ's
body and the church) and "the counsel of peace shall be between
them both" — between the priestly and kingly offices, reconciled
in Christ alone.

Structure:
- The Eighth Vision: Four Chariots from Bronze Mountains (vv.1-3)
- The Interpretation: Four Spirits of Heaven (vv.4-8)
- The Symbolic Crowning of Joshua (vv.9-11)
- The Branch Prophecy: Priest-King (vv.12-13)
- The Crown as a Memorial (v.14)
- Those Far Off Shall Build (v.15)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Revelation  | 6       | 1-8                | The four horsemen echo Zechariah's four colored chariot-teams
v.5              | Psalm       | 104     | 4                  | "He makes his messengers winds, his ministers a flaming fire." (ESV)
v.12             | John        | 19      | 5                  | "Behold the man!" (ESV) — Pilate unknowingly echoing Zechariah's Messianic declaration
v.12             | Ephesians   | 2       | 19-22              | The church as God's temple, built by Christ
v.13             | Psalm       | 110     | 4                  | "You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek." (ESV) — the priest-king
v.13             | Hebrews     | 4       | 14                 | 5:10 — Christ as both King and High Priest
v.15             | Ephesians   | 2       | 13                 | "But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ." (ESV)
v.15             | 1 Peter     | 2       | 5                  | Believers as "living stones" built up into a spiritual house

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "mountains of brass" (KJV) / "mountains of bronze" (ESV) — Hebrew "nekhoshet" = bronze/copper. Bronze represents divine judgment throughout Scripture (the bronze altar, the bronze serpent). These mountains are the gates of God's court of justice, from which judgment proceeds.
v.3              | KJV         | "grisled and bay" (KJV) / "dappled... strong" (ESV) — Hebrew "beruddim amutsim." KJV takes these as two descriptions of the fourth team; ESV applies "strong" as a quality of all the horses. The colors may represent different aspects of divine judgment.
v.12             | KJV         | "The BRANCH" — Hebrew "tsemach" (same as 3:8). "He shall grow up out of his place" — "branch out from his place" (ESV). He rises from apparent insignificance to universal rule.
v.13             | KJV         | "the counsel of peace shall be between them both" — "Them both" likely refers to the two offices (priest and king) unified in one Person without conflict. Alternately: between the Branch and the LORD. Either way, perfect harmony between authority and intercession in the Messiah.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God is sovereign over all nations (vv.1-8). The chariots go north, south,
   east — covering the entire earth. No empire is beyond God's reach. No
   political power operates outside His governance. Whatever threatens you,
   God has agents already dispatched to deal with it.

2. Christ is both your King AND your Priest (v.13). He rules with authority
   AND intercedes with compassion. He commands AND He understands. You are
   governed by a ruler who also represents you before God. No tension — only
   peace between His throne and His altar.

3. "Those far off shall come and build" (v.15). If you feel like an outsider
   — far from God, far from the inner circle, far from worthiness — hear
   this: you are INVITED to build. The "far off" ones are included. In
   Christ, there are no outsiders.

4. God's Spirit finds "rest" when justice is done (v.8). Justice is not
   optional in God's economy — it is what brings Him peace. Work for
   justice. Pursue righteousness. When wrongs are righted, God's Spirit
   rests.

7.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 7
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A delegation asks about continuing fast days, and God responds that their fasting was self-serving — what He truly requires is justice, mercy, and compassion, which their fathers refused and reaped exile.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — The Question of Fasting and
the Demand for Justice
Key Themes: Empty ritual versus genuine obedience, self-centered
religion exposed, God's true requirements (justice, mercy,
compassion), the hard heart that refuses to hear, the
consequences of persistent disobedience

Historical Context: Two years after the night visions (now the
fourth year of Darius, 518 BC), a delegation from Bethel arrives
with a practical question: should they continue the fasts
commemorating Jerusalem's fall? They had fasted in the fifth
month (the Temple's destruction) and the seventh month
(Gedaliah's assassination) for seventy years. Now that the
Temple is being rebuilt, are these fasts still required?

God's answer is devastating: "When you fasted... did you really
fast for ME?" (v.5). Their fasting was self-referential — about
THEIR grief, THEIR national identity, THEIR religious routine.
It was never truly about God. Similarly, "when you eat and
drink, do you not eat for YOURSELVES?" (v.6). Whether fasting
or feasting, they were self-centered.

God then reminds them what He actually requires: true justice,
mercy, compassion — not ritual. Their fathers refused this and
made their hearts like "adamant stone" (diamond-hard). The
consequence: God scattered them and desolated their land. The
lesson for this generation is clear: don't repeat the pattern.
Ritual without righteousness is worthless.

Structure:
- The Delegation's Question: Should We Still Fast? (vv.1-3)
- God's Probing Response: Was It Really for Me? (vv.4-6)
- The True Requirement: Justice and Mercy (vv.7-10)
- The Fathers' Stubborn Refusal (vv.11-12)
- The Terrible Consequence: As I Called, They Refused (vv.13-14)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.5              | Isaiah      | 58      | 3-7                | God's exposé of empty fasting: "Is not this the fast that I choose: to loose the bonds of wickedness?" (ESV)
v.5              | Matthew     | 6       | 16-18              | Jesus' teaching on fasting for God's approval, not human attention
v.9              | Micah       | 6       | 8                  | "What does the LORD require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God?" (ESV)
v.10             | James       | 1       | 27                 | "Religion that is pure and undefiled before God the Father is this: to visit orphans and widows in their affliction." (ESV)
v.12             | Hebrews     | 3       | 7-8                | "Do not harden your hearts as in the rebellion." (ESV)
v.13             | Proverbs    | 1       | 24-28              | "Then they will call upon me, but I will not answer" (ESV) — the consequence of rejecting wisdom
v.14             | Deuteronomy | 28      | 64                 | The scattering among nations as covenant consequence for disobedience

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3              | KJV         | "separating myself" (KJV) / "abstain" (ESV) — Hebrew "hinnazar" = to consecrate oneself by abstaining (same root as "Nazirite"). The fasting involved specific acts of self- denial — but the motivation was wrong.
v.11             | KJV         | "pulled away the shoulder" (KJV) / "turned a stubborn shoulder" (ESV) — Hebrew "nathan kateph sorerot" = literally "gave a turning/rebellious shoulder." The image of an ox refusing the yoke — turning its shoulder away from the load. Willful resistance to God's direction.
v.12             | KJV         | "adamant stone" (KJV) / "diamond-hard" (ESV) — Hebrew "shamir." Used elsewhere for the hardest cutting stone. The heart became impenetrable — no word could break through. A self-inflicted condition with devastating consequences.
v.14             | KJV         | "pleasant land" — Hebrew "eretz chemdah" = the land of delight/desire. The Promised Land — so beautiful, so blessed — made desolate by disobedience. The land reflects the spiritual state of its people.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Did ye fast unto ME?" (v.5). Examine your spiritual disciplines. Is your
   prayer life about connecting with GOD or about feeling religious? Is your
   church attendance for HIM or for your social identity? The crucial
   question is not whether you do spiritual things but WHO they are actually
   for.

2. God wants justice and mercy, not just worship services (vv.9-10). You
   cannot sing on Sunday and oppress on Monday. True worship produces just
   behavior. If your religion doesn't change how you treat the vulnerable,
   it isn't real religion (James 1:27).

3. A hard heart is chosen, not inherited (v.12). They MADE their hearts
   diamond-hard. Hardness is a decision — repeated refusals to listen
   calcify into inability to hear. Every time you dismiss God's conviction,
   your heart gets a little harder. Respond NOW while your heart is still
   soft.

4. God's patience has limits (v.13). He called; they refused. Then THEY
   called; HE refused. This is sobering. God is patient — but persistent
   rejection exhausts even divine patience. Don't presume on grace. Respond
   to God while He is still calling.

5. Disobedience desolates what was once beautiful (v.14). The "pleasant
   land" became wasteland. Sin ALWAYS desolates. What was once flourishing —
   your marriage, your joy, your calling — can become wasteland through
   persistent disobedience. Choose repentance before desolation.

8.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 8
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God promises to return to Zion with jealous love — old men and women will sit in Jerusalem's streets while children play, fasts will become feasts, and nations will seek the LORD because "God is with you."

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — Ten Promises of Restoration
and Universal Blessing
Key Themes: God's jealousy for Zion, the future Jerusalem of
peace and prosperity, the reversal of former judgment, ethical
requirements for the blessed community, fasts transformed to
feasts, the nations drawn to God through Israel

Historical Context: Chapter 8 answers the fasting question of
chapter 7 with overwhelming promise. Where chapter 7 warned
through history's failures, chapter 8 soars with future hope.
Ten oracles (each introduced by "Thus saith the LORD of hosts")
paint a picture of Jerusalem's glorious future: elderly people
peacefully aging in the streets, children playing safely, the
remnant gathered from east and west, and the nations streaming
to seek the LORD.

This chapter is one of the most encouraging in the Minor
Prophets. To a tiny, discouraged remnant, God says: what seems
impossible to you is NOT impossible to Me (v.6). He will bring
His people home. He will bless their land. Their fasts will
become feasts. And the nations will cling to Israel, saying
"God is with you." The chapter ends with one of the most
stunning prophecies of Gentile inclusion in the OT.

Structure:
- God's Jealousy for Zion (vv.1-2)
- The Faithful City: Elderly and Children at Peace (vv.3-5)
- Nothing is Impossible for God (v.6)
- The Gathering from East and West (vv.7-8)
- "Let Your Hands Be Strong" (vv.9-13)
- God's Purpose to Do Good (vv.14-15)
- Ethical Requirements: Truth, Justice, Peace (vv.16-17)
- Fasts Become Feasts (vv.18-19)
- The Nations Seek the LORD (vv.20-23)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.3              | Revelation  | 21      | 2-3                | The holy city, New Jerusalem, where God dwells with His people
v.5              | Isaiah      | 11      | 6-9                | The peaceable kingdom where children are safe even among wild animals
v.6              | Genesis     | 18      | 14                 | "Is anything too hard for the LORD?" (ESV)
v.7              | Isaiah      | 43      | 5-6                | "I will bring your offspring from the east, and from the west I will gather you." (ESV)
v.8              | Jeremiah    | 31      | 33                 | "I will be their God, and they shall be my people" (ESV) — the covenant formula
v.13             | Genesis     | 12      | 2-3                | "I will bless you... and you shall be a blessing." (ESV)
v.16             | Ephesians   | 4       | 25                 | "Having put away falsehood, let each one of you speak the truth with his neighbor." (ESV)
v.23             | Isaiah      | 2       | 2-3                | "Many peoples shall come, and say: 'Come, let us go up to the mountain of the LORD.'" (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "jealous" — Hebrew "qana" = zealous, jealous with burning passion. God's jealousy for Zion is not petty envy but fierce protective love. He will fight for what is His.
v.6              | KJV         | "marvellous" — Hebrew "yippale" = wonderful, extraordinary, hard to believe. The root "pala" is used of God's miracles. What is "too wonderful" for man is ordinary for God.
v.12             | KJV         | "seed of peace" (KJV) / "sowing of peace" (ESV) — Hebrew "zera hashalom." Some translations read this as the vine being called "peaceful" or that peace will be sown. The agricultural metaphor: God sows peace as a seed that produces a harvest of prosperity.
v.23             | KJV         | "ten men" — A round number representing completeness. From EVERY language — universality. The ratio (ten Gentiles to one Jew) shows the overwhelming Gentile response to God's work through Israel.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God's vision includes PLAY and REST (v.5). His picture of the blessed
   future is not all work and worship — it includes children playing. God
   values joy, leisure, and human flourishing. Don't reduce Christian life
   to duty alone. God wants His streets filled with laughter.

2. "Should it also be marvellous in MY eyes?" (v.6). Stop limiting God by
   your own sense of what's possible. Your impossibility is His Tuesday
   morning. If the promise seems too good to be true, remember: you're
   measuring by human standards. God is not limited by your measurements.

3. "Fear not, let your hands be strong" (vv.13, 15). Faith produces action.
   God says: I WILL bless you — therefore work with confidence. Don't wait
   for blessing to arrive before you act. Work in faith, knowing God has
   purposed good for you.

4. "Love truth and peace" (v.19). These are the twin marks of God's people.
   Not truth without peace (harshness). Not peace without truth
   (compromise). Both together — the mark of mature spiritual community.

5. "God is with you" (v.23). The ultimate testimony that draws the nations
   is not our programs or our arguments but the manifest PRESENCE of God
   among His people. Live in such a way that others say: "God is clearly
   with you — let us come too."

------------------------------------

9.ESV:
1. The oracle of the word of the LORD is against the land of Hadrach and Damascus is its resting place. For the LORD has an eye on mankind and on all the tribes of Israel,
2. and on Hamath also, which borders on it, Tyre and Sidon, though they are very wise.
3. Tyre has built herself a rampart and heaped up silver like dust, and fine gold like the mud of the streets.
4. But behold, the Lord will strip her of her possessions and strike down her power on the sea, and she shall be devoured by fire.
5. Ashkelon shall see it, and be afraid; Gaza too, and shall writhe in anguish; Ekron also, because its hopes are confounded. The king shall perish from Gaza; Ashkelon shall be uninhabited;
6. a mixed people shall dwell in Ashdod, and I will cut off the pride of Philistia.
7. I will take away its blood from its mouth, and its abominations from between its teeth; it too shall be a remnant for our God; it shall be like a clan in Judah, and Ekron shall be like the Jebusites.
8. Then I will encamp at my house as a guard, so that none shall march to and fro; no oppressor shall again march over them, for now I see with my own eyes.
9. Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion! Shout aloud, O daughter of Jerusalem! Behold, your king is coming to you; righteous and having salvation is he, humble and mounted on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.
10. I will cut off the chariot from Ephraim and the war horse from Jerusalem; and the battle bow shall be cut off, and he shall speak peace to the nations; his rule shall be from sea to sea, and from the River to the ends of the earth.
11. As for you also, because of the blood of my covenant with you, I will set your prisoners free from the waterless pit.
12. Return to your stronghold, O prisoners of hope; today I declare that I will restore to you double.
13. For I have bent Judah as my bow; I have made Ephraim its arrow. I will stir up your sons, O Zion, against your sons, O Greece, and wield you like a warrior’s sword.
14. Then the LORD will appear over them, and his arrow will go forth like lightning; the Lord GOD will sound the trumpet and will march forth in the whirlwinds of the south.
15. The LORD of hosts will protect them, and they shall devour, and tread down the sling stones, and they shall drink and roar as if drunk with wine, and be full like a bowl, drenched like the corners of the altar.
16. On that day the LORD their God will save them, as the flock of his people; for like the jewels of a crown they shall shine on his land.
17. For how great is his goodness, and how great his beauty! Grain shall make the young men flourish, and new wine the young women.

9.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 9
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An oracle against surrounding nations (Hadrach, Damascus, Tyre, Philistia) leads to the Messianic prophecy of Zion's King coming humble and riding on a donkey, bringing peace to the nations.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — The Coming King and the
Judgment of Surrounding Nations
Key Themes: Judgment on Israel's enemies, the triumphal entry
of the humble King, the Prince of Peace, prisoners of hope set
free, God as warrior and protector, Messianic kingdom dominion

Historical Context: Chapter 9 marks a major transition in
Zechariah. Chapters 1-8 were dated visions and oracles during
Temple reconstruction. Chapters 9-14 are undated "burdens"
(oracles) dealing with the distant future — Messianic in focus
and apocalyptic in tone. Many scholars call chapters 9-14
"Second Zechariah," though the traditional view holds
Zechariah as the single author writing at different periods.

The chapter opens with God's judgment sweeping through Israel's
neighbors: Hadrach, Damascus, Hamath, Tyre, Sidon, and the
Philistine cities. This passage was strikingly fulfilled by
Alexander the Great's campaign in 333-332 BC, which followed
exactly this geographic path — yet miraculously bypassed
Jerusalem (v.8).

Then comes the astonishing verse 9: the King enters Jerusalem
not on a war horse but on a donkey. He is just, having
salvation, and HUMBLE. This was literally fulfilled when Jesus
rode into Jerusalem on Palm Sunday (Matt 21:1-9). The chapter
then stretches to the Messianic kingdom's universal dominion
"from sea to sea" (v.10).

Structure:
- Judgment on Damascus, Hamath, Tyre, and Sidon (vv.1-4)
- Judgment on the Philistine Cities (vv.5-7)
- God's Protection of His House (v.8)
- The Triumphal Entry of the Humble King (v.9)
- The Prince of Peace: Dominion to the Ends of Earth (v.10)
- Prisoners of Hope Set Free (vv.11-12)
- God's People as His Weapon Against Greece (vv.13-15)
- The LORD Saves His Flock (vv.16-17)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.9              | Matthew     | 21      | 1-9                | The triumphal entry: Jesus fulfills this prophecy literally, riding on a donkey
v.9              | John        | 12      | 15                 | "Fear not, daughter of Zion; behold, your king is coming, sitting on a donkey's colt!" (ESV)
v.9              | Philippians | 2       | 5-8                | Christ's humility: "he humbled himself" (ESV) — the nature of this King
v.10             | Psalm       | 72      | 8                  | "May he have dominion from sea to sea, and from the River to the ends of the earth!" (ESV)
v.10             | Isaiah      | 9       | 6-7                | "Prince of Peace... of the increase of his government and of peace there will be no end." (ESV)
v.11             | Exodus      | 24      | 8                  | "Behold the blood of the covenant that the LORD has made with you." (ESV) — Moses sprinkling the people with blood at Sinai
v.11             | Hebrews     | 13      | 20                 | "The blood of the eternal covenant." (ESV)
v.12             | Isaiah      | 61      | 7                  | "Instead of your shame there shall be a double portion." (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "burden" (KJV) / "oracle" (ESV) — Hebrew "massa" = a weighty utterance, a prophetic pronouncement. It carries the sense of heaviness — God's word lands with weight.
v.9              | KJV         | "having salvation" — Hebrew "nosha" = passive participle: "saved" or "endowed with salvation." The King does not merely BRING salvation — He EMBODIES it. He is Himself the saved/saving one. Some translate "victorious."
v.9              | KJV         | "lowly" (KJV) / "humble" (ESV) — Hebrew "ani" = afflicted, poor, humble. The same word used of Moses (Num 12:3) and the Messiah in Isaiah 53. This King conquers not by pride and power but by meekness and lowliness.
v.12             | KJV         | "prisoners of hope" — Hebrew "asirey hatikvah." They are imprisoned, yes — but their prison is HOPE. What confines them is not despair but expectation. Even in the pit, hope holds them.
v.13             | KJV         | "sons of Greece" — Hebrew "bene Yavan" = sons of Ionia/ Greece. A remarkable prediction of the Maccabean conflict with the Greek/Seleucid empire (168-165 BC), centuries before it occurred.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Your King comes humble (v.9). In a world that worships power, Jesus
   arrives on a donkey. The kingdom of God is not advanced by force, wealth,
   or political influence — but by humility, service, and sacrificial love.
   Follow your King's example.

2. "Prisoners of hope" (v.12). If you are in a pit today — a season of
   suffering, loss, or waiting — know that you are not a prisoner of despair
   but a prisoner of HOPE. Hope is what holds you. And God promises double
   restoration. Your suffering will not be wasted.

3. Peace is spoken, not forced (v.10). "He shall SPEAK peace to the
   nations." The Messiah's dominion expands through His word — through the
   gospel proclaimed. True and lasting peace comes not through military
   conquest but through the transforming word of Christ.

4. "By the blood of thy covenant" (v.11). Freedom comes at a price. Your
   liberation from the waterless pit was purchased by blood — ultimately the
   blood of Christ. Freedom is never free. Receive it with gratitude and
   live worthy of the cost.

5. You are "stones of a crown" (v.16). God does not see you as expendable
   sheep — He sees you as crown jewels. You are His treasure, lifted high,
   displayed with honor. Live with the dignity of someone treasured by the
   King.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Zechariah Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/zechariah/

David Guzik — Zechariah 9 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/zechariah-9/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

10.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 10
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God promises to strengthen Judah and save Ephraim, gathering His scattered people from among the nations — they will be as mighty as before, and He will whistle for them and redeem them.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — The Restoration of the
Scattered Flock
Key Themes: God as the true source of rain and blessing, false
shepherds condemned, God's anger against worthless leaders,
the cornerstone/tent peg/battle bow from Judah, the regathering
of Ephraim and Judah, divine redemption and strengthening

Historical Context: Chapter 10 continues the theme of Israel's
restoration, contrasting true divine provision with false
sources. The people had turned to "idols" and "diviners" for
guidance (v.2), and their leaders ("shepherds") had failed them.
God is angry at these worthless shepherds who left the flock
wandering and afflicted.

But God Himself will care for His flock (v.3). From Judah will
come the cornerstone, the tent peg, the battle bow — all
Messianic images of stability, security, and victory. God will
strengthen both Judah (south) and Joseph/Ephraim (north) — the
entire divided nation reunited under His care.

The chapter contains some of the most tender restoration
language in the prophets: "they shall be as though I had not
cast them off" (v.6). God's restoration is so complete that the
shame of exile is erased. He "whistles" for them (v.8) — the
shepherd's call to gather scattered sheep — and brings them
home until "place shall not be found for them" (v.10).

Structure:
- Ask the LORD for Rain, Not Idols (vv.1-2)
- God's Anger Against the Shepherds (v.3)
- From Judah: Cornerstone, Tent Peg, Battle Bow (v.4)
- Mighty Warriors Because the LORD is With Them (v.5)
- The Full Restoration of Judah and Ephraim (vv.6-7)
- God Whistles for His Scattered People (vv.8-10)
- The Sea of Trouble Parted (v.11)
- Strengthened and Walking in His Name (v.12)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Matthew     | 9       | 36                 | "When he saw the crowds, he had compassion for them, because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd." (ESV)
v.2              | Ezekiel     | 34      | 5                  | "So they were scattered, because there was no shepherd." (ESV)
v.4              | Isaiah      | 28      | 16                 | "Behold, I am the one who has laid as a foundation in Zion, a stone, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone." (ESV)
v.4              | Isaiah      | 22      | 23                 | "And I will fasten him like a peg in a secure place." (ESV) — the tent peg of stability
v.6              | Romans      | 8       | 1                  | "There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus." (ESV) — as though never rejected
v.8              | John        | 10      | 27                 | "My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me." (ESV)
v.10             | Isaiah      | 11      | 11-12              | God's regathering from Assyria, Egypt, and the nations
v.11             | Exodus      | 14      | 21-22              | The original parting of the sea—God will do it again

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "latter rain" — Hebrew "malqosh" = the spring rain (March-April) that brings crops to maturity before harvest. Without it, the harvest fails. God controls the rain — it is His to give or withhold.
v.2              | KJV         | "idols" (KJV) / "household gods" (ESV) — Hebrew "teraphim" = household idols used for divination. Small figurines consulted for guidance instead of God. A persistent temptation in Israel's history.
v.4              | KJV         | "corner" / "cornerstone" — Hebrew "pinnah" = corner, cornerstone, chief. The stone that determines the alignment of the entire building. Everything is oriented by this stone.
v.4              | KJV         | "nail" / "tent peg" — Hebrew "yated" = a peg or stake. In a tent culture, the peg held everything in place. Remove the peg and the tent collapses. Messiah is the stabilizing fixture of God's people.
v.8              | KJV         | "hiss" (KJV) / "whistle" (ESV) — Hebrew "sharaq" = to whistle, to pipe. Used of shepherds calling flocks and beekeepers summoning bees (Isaiah 7:18). God's call is specific and personal.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Ask the LORD for rain" (v.1). Whatever you need — provision, guidance,
   refreshing — go to GOD first, not substitutes. Not horoscopes, not
   self-help, not worry. Ask the LORD. He makes the storm clouds. He gives
   the showers. He alone.

2. Bad leadership causes wandering (v.2). If you are in a place of spiritual
   confusion or wandering, consider: who are you following? False shepherds
   produce scattered sheep. Seek leaders who point to Christ, not to
   themselves.

3. Christ is your cornerstone, tent peg, and battle bow (v.4). He gives you
   alignment (cornerstone — you know which way to build). He gives stability
   (tent peg — you won't collapse). He gives victory (battle bow — you can
   overcome). Everything you need comes "from Him."

4. "As though I had not cast them off" (v.6). God's grace doesn't just
   forgive — it ERASES the shame. You are not a "recovering sinner" forever
   marked by failure. In God's eyes, you are restored so completely it's as
   if the rejection never happened. Stop carrying shame God has already
   removed.

5. God whistles for you (v.8). When you are scattered and far away, God
   doesn't shout in anger — He whistles. A familiar, tender call that you
   recognize. Listen for His whistle today. He is calling you home.

11.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 11
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Zechariah enacts a parable of two shepherd staffs — Favor and Union — breaking them to symbolize God's rejected covenant and the breaking of brotherhood between Judah and Israel, valued at thirty pieces of silver.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — The Rejected Shepherd and
the Thirty Pieces of Silver
Key Themes: The Good Shepherd rejected, the flock doomed to
slaughter, the breaking of covenant, thirty pieces of silver
(the price of Messiah's betrayal), the foolish shepherd who
destroys the flock, judgment for rejecting the true Shepherd

Historical Context: Chapter 11 is one of the most remarkable
prophetic passages in the OT. Zechariah enacts a prophetic drama
as a shepherd over a "flock doomed to slaughter." He takes two
staffs — "Beauty" (Favor) and "Bands" (Union) — representing
God's gracious covenant and the unity of the people. But the
flock despises the shepherd, and the shepherd breaks both staffs
in sequence: first Favor (the covenant annulled) then Union
(national brotherhood dissolved).

The most astonishing detail: when asked for his wages, the
shepherd receives THIRTY PIECES OF SILVER — described with
bitter irony as "a goodly price" (the price of a gored slave,
Exodus 21:32). God says: "Cast it to the potter" — and the
prophet throws the silver into the Temple, to the potter. This
was fulfilled with eerie precision when Judas received thirty
silver pieces for betraying Christ (Matt 26:15) and later threw
them into the Temple, after which the priests used them to buy
the potter's field (Matt 27:3-10).

Structure:
- The Devastation of the Land (vv.1-3)
- The Shepherd of the Doomed Flock (vv.4-7)
- The Shepherd Rejected by the Flock (vv.8-9)
- The Staff "Beauty" Broken: Covenant Annulled (vv.10-11)
- Thirty Pieces of Silver: The Shepherd's Price (vv.12-13)
- The Staff "Bands" Broken: Brotherhood Dissolved (v.14)
- The Rise of the Foolish Shepherd (vv.15-17)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.7              | Ezekiel     | 34      | 23                 | "I will set up over them one shepherd, my servant David" (ESV) — God's solution for bad shepherds
v.12             | Matthew     | 26      | 15                 | "They paid him thirty pieces of silver" (ESV) — Judas's price for Christ
v.13             | Matthew     | 27      | 3-10               | Judas throwing the silver into the Temple; the priests buying the potter's field
v.13             | Exodus      | 21      | 32                 | Thirty shekels: the price of a dead slave
v.10             | Romans      | 11      | 1-10               | The partial hardening/rejection of Israel after rejecting Messiah
v.14             | Ezekiel     | 37      | 15-22              | The two sticks (Judah and Israel) that will one day be reunited
v.16             | John        | 10      | 12-13              | "He who is a hired hand and not a shepherd... cares nothing for the sheep" (ESV) — the false shepherd who abandons the flock
v.16             | 2 Thessalonians | 2       | 3-4                | The man of lawlessness — the ultimate "foolish shepherd."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.7              | KJV         | "Beauty" (KJV) / "Favor" (ESV) — Hebrew "no'am" = grace, pleasantness, beauty, favor. Represents God's gracious covenant relationship with His people.
v.7              | KJV         | "Bands" (KJV) / "Union" (ESV) — Hebrew "choblim" = cords, bands, binding together. Represents the brotherhood that holds Judah and Israel together as one people.
v.12             | KJV         | "thirty pieces of silver" — Hebrew "sheloshim kasef." The exact compensation for a slave (Exod 21:32). The insult is deliberate: the Good Shepherd is valued at the cheapest possible rate. This is contempt, not payment.
v.13             | KJV         | "the potter" — Hebrew "yotser." Why "the potter"? Perhaps connected to the potter's field (Matt 27:7) or to God as the Potter who forms vessels (Jer 18:1-6). The connection between Zechariah's prophecy and the NT fulfillment is remarkably precise.
v.17             | KJV         | "idol shepherd" (KJV) / "worthless shepherd" (ESV) — Hebrew "ro'i ha'elil" = shepherd of nothingness/worthless- ness. "Elil" is related to idols (worthless things). A shepherd who is himself an idol — empty, powerless, and destructive.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Rejecting the Good Shepherd has consequences (vv.8-10). When Israel
   despised Christ, they received oppressive leaders instead. Whom you
   reject determines whom you receive. If you reject Christ's gentle rule,
   you will end up under harsher masters — sin, self, or the world.

2. God was valued at slave-price (v.12). Thirty pieces of silver — that's
   what the world thought Jesus was worth. How do YOU value Christ? Is He
   slave-price to you — an afterthought, a convenience? Or is He the Pearl
   of great price for whom you sell everything? (Matt 13:46)

3. When "Beauty" is broken, grace departs (v.10). God's favor is not
   automatic. When persistently rejected, He withdraws His gracious
   protection. Don't take God's patience for granted. Covenant blessings
   have covenant conditions.

4. When "Bands" is broken, unity dissolves (v.14). Rejection of Christ
   fractures community. Without the true Shepherd, the flock divides against
   itself. Unity among God's people is maintained by shared submission to
   Christ. When He is rejected, brotherhood disintegrates.

5. The foolish shepherd devours what the Good Shepherd would feed (v.16).
   False leaders consume the people they should serve. Be discerning about
   who shepherds you. A true shepherd heals, seeks, and feeds. A false one
   exploits, abandons, and devours.

12.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 12
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God will defend Jerusalem against all nations, pour out a spirit of grace and supplication, and they will look on Him whom they have pierced — mourning as for an only son in that day.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — Jerusalem's Deliverance and
National Mourning Over the Pierced One
Key Themes: Jerusalem as an immovable burden stone, God's
supernatural defense of His people, the spirit of grace poured
out, Israel looking on Him whom they pierced, national
repentance and mourning, the deity of Christ implied

Historical Context: Chapter 12 begins the second "burden"
(oracle) of the final section (chs. 12-14), dealing with the
end times and Israel's final deliverance. The chapter describes
a future siege against Jerusalem by "all the nations" — a
scenario that has no complete historical fulfillment yet. God
introduces Himself with creation-credentials (v.1): the One who
stretched the heavens, founded the earth, and forms the human
spirit. This is not a minor prophet speaking — this is the
Creator of the universe.

Jerusalem becomes a "cup of staggering" and a "heavy stone" for
all nations. Those who try to move it will be injured. God
supernaturally defends Jerusalem — even the weakest will be
"like David," and David's house will be "like God." Then comes
the climactic verse 10: God pours out His Spirit, and Israel
looks on "me whom they have pierced" — mourning for Him as for
an only son. The identification is staggering: GOD says "they
pierced ME," yet they mourn for "HIM." This implies the deity of
the one pierced — God and yet distinct. This is Christ crucified.

Structure:
- God's Credentials: Creator of Heaven, Earth, and Spirit (v.1)
- Jerusalem: A Cup of Staggering for the Nations (v.2)
- Jerusalem: A Heavy Stone That Injures All Who Lift It (v.3)
- God Strikes the Enemies with Confusion (vv.4-5)
- Judah as Fire Among Wood (v.6)
- God's Salvation: The Weak Made Strong (vv.7-8)
- God Seeks to Destroy All Opposing Nations (v.9)
- The Spirit Poured Out: Looking on the Pierced One (v.10)
- The Great Mourning — Every Family Apart (vv.11-14)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
