-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Isaiah      | 42      | 5                  | "Thus says God, the LORD, who created the heavens and stretched them out" (ESV)
v.3              | Joel        | 3       | 2                  | "I will gather all the nations... and I will enter into judgment with them there" (ESV)
v.8              | 1 Samuel    | 17      | 50                 | David defeating Goliath — the paradigm of weak made mighty by God
v.10             | John        | 19      | 37                 | "They will look on him whom they have pierced" (ESV) — John quotes this at the crucifixion
v.10             | Revelation  | 1       | 7                  | "Every eye will see him, even those who pierced him, and all tribes of the earth will wail on account of him" (ESV)
v.10             | Romans      | 11      | 26                 | "And in this way all Israel will be saved" (ESV) — the future national turning prophesied here
v.11             | 2 Chronicles | 35      | 22-25              | The mourning for King Josiah at Megiddo
v.10             | Acts        | 2       | 37                 | "They were cut to the heart" (ESV) — an early foreshadowing at Pentecost

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "cup of trembling" (KJV) / "cup of staggering" (ESV) — Hebrew "saph ra'al" = a bowl/cup of reeling/staggering. Jerusalem becomes an intoxicating trap for the nations — they drink and cannot stand. Those who try to consume Jerusalem are themselves consumed.
v.3              | KJV         | "burdensome stone" (KJV) / "heavy stone" (ESV) — Hebrew "even ma'amasah" = a stone of heavy burden. Like trying to lift a massive boulder — those who attempt it are cut and injured. Jerusalem cannot be moved by human effort.
v.10             | KJV         | "pierced" — Hebrew "daqar" = to thrust through, to stab. Used of a fatal wound (as in Num 25:8). This is not a minor injury — it is a death-blow. GOD says they thrust Him through. The crucifixion is described 500+ years before it happens.
v.10             | KJV         | The pronoun shift: "look upon ME" (first person — God speaking) to "mourn for HIM" (third person — as for another). This theological tension points to the mystery of the Trinity: the One pierced is GOD, yet mourned as a distinct Person. The Father and Son are one, yet distinct.
v.11             | KJV         | "Hadadrimmon" — A place in the Valley of Megiddo (Jezreel Valley). Likely the location of mourning for King Josiah, killed in battle (609 BC). The greatest grief Israel ever experienced becomes the standard for future repentance.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. God is both Creator AND Redeemer (v.1). The same God who stretched out
   the cosmos is the one who enters history to save Jerusalem. He is not too
   big for your problems — the maker of galaxies attends to your city, your
   life, your heart.

2. Don't fight against what God has planted (v.3). Jerusalem is immovable
   because God set it there. In your life, certain things are
   GOD-established — don't try to uproot them, and don't let others move
   what God has placed.

3. God's strength shines through your weakness (v.8). In God's economy, the
   feeblest becomes David. Stop disqualifying yourself because of weakness.
   God's power is not limited by your frailty — it is displayed through it.

4. "They shall look upon me whom they have pierced" (v.10). One day, every
   human being will see the wounds of Christ and understand what happened at
   Calvary. For some it will be repentant mourning (leading to salvation).
   For others, it will be the terror of recognition. Look NOW — while
   repentance is still possible.

5. True repentance is personal and grievous (vv.12-14). Each family mourns
   APART — individually, personally. Corporate revival begins with
   individual brokenness. Don't hide in the crowd. Let your repentance be
   personal, specific, and deep.

13.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 13
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A fountain will be opened for sin and uncleanness, idols and false prophets will be cut off, and God will strike the Shepherd — scattering the sheep — then refine a remnant through fire who will call on His name.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — The Fountain for Sin, the
Smitten Shepherd, and the Refined Remnant
Key Themes: A fountain opened for cleansing from sin, the end
of idolatry and false prophecy, the shepherd struck by God's
own sword, the sheep scattered, the remnant refined through
fire, covenant restoration

Historical Context: Chapter 13 flows directly from chapter 12's
vision of mourning over the pierced one. The mourning of 12:10-14
leads to the CLEANSING of 13:1. Repentance produces purification.
"In that day" a fountain is opened — not for ritual washing but
for SIN and UNCLEANNESS. The blood of Christ is this fountain,
available for all who mourn over what their sins have done to Him.

The chapter then describes the purging of false religion (vv.2-6)
before reaching its climax in verse 7: "Awake, O sword, against
my shepherd." God HIMSELF calls the sword against His own
shepherd — "the man that is my fellow" (literally "my associate,
my equal"). This is God commanding the death of one who is His
own equal — the crucifixion seen from the divine perspective.
Jesus quoted this verse on the night of His arrest (Matt 26:31):
"I will smite the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock shall be
scattered."

The aftermath (vv.8-9) describes a devastating judgment where
two-thirds perish but one-third is refined. The refined remnant
emerges with a renewed covenant: "I will say, It is my people;
and they shall say, The LORD is my God."

Structure:
- The Fountain Opened for Sin and Uncleanness (v.1)
- The Purging of Idols and False Prophets (vv.2-6)
- The Sword Against the Shepherd (v.7)
- The Sheep Scattered and the Little Ones (v.7b)
- The Two-Thirds Cut Off (v.8)
- The One-Third Refined: Covenant Restored (v.9)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | 1 John      | 1       | 7                  | "The blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin" (ESV)
v.1              | Revelation  | 7       | 14                 | "They have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb" (ESV)
v.1              | Ezekiel     | 36      | 25                 | "I will sprinkle clean water on you, and you shall be clean" (ESV)
v.7              | Matthew     | 26      | 31                 | Jesus quotes this verse: "I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered" (ESV)
v.7              | Mark        | 14      | 27                 | Jesus again quoting Zechariah 13:7 on the way to the Mount of Olives
v.7              | Isaiah      | 53      | 4,                 | 10 — "Stricken, smitten by God... it was the will of the LORD to crush him" (ESV) — the same divine initiative in the shepherd's death
v.9              | 1 Peter     | 1       | 6-7                | "The tested genuineness of your faith... though tested by fire" (ESV)
v.9              | Hosea       | 2       | 23                 | "I will say... 'You are my people'; and he shall say, 'You are my God'" (ESV)

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "fountain" — Hebrew "maqor" = a spring, a source. Not stagnant water but a living, flowing spring. It is OPENED — made accessible, available, free. The fountain does not run dry. Christ's atoning blood is an ever-flowing spring.
v.6              | KJV         | "wounds in thine hands" (KJV) / "wounds on your back" (ESV) — Hebrew "makkot ben yadeyka" = wounds between your hands (or between your arms). KJV reads "in thy hands"; ESV reads "on your back" (between the shoulders). The Hebrew allows both readings. Either way, the wounds are visible and demand explanation.
v.7              | KJV         | "my fellow" — Hebrew "amiti" = my associate, my companion, my kinsman, my equal. This is the ONLY occurrence of this word in relation to God's shepherd. It means the shepherd stands in the closest possible relationship to God — He is God's equal. This is a claim of deity for the Messiah.
v.7              | KJV         | "smite" — Hebrew "hak" (imperative of nakah) = to strike, to kill. This is a COMMAND — God orders the sword to strike. The cross was not an accident but a divine decree. "It pleased the LORD to bruise him" (Isa 53:10).
v.9              | KJV         | "refine" — Hebrew "tsaraph" = to smelt, to purify by fire. Used of refining precious metals. The purpose is not destruction but PURIFICATION. What survives the fire is pure gold — the genuine remnant of faith.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "A fountain opened for sin" (v.1). Not a fountain you must earn access to
   — it is OPENED. Freely available. Abundantly flowing. If you have sin and
   uncleanness (and you do), there is a fountain. Come to it. The blood of
   Christ cleanses ALL sin — not some, but all.

2. "The man that is my fellow" (v.7). The shepherd whom God strikes is God's
   own EQUAL. Jesus is not merely a good man martyred — He is God's equal,
   struck by God's own sword. The cross is not a tragedy that God allowed;
   it is a sacrifice that God COMMANDED. The Father's love for you is
   measured by what it cost Him: His equal, His Son.

3. "Smite the shepherd, and the sheep shall be scattered" (v.7). Jesus
   quoted this to prepare His disciples for His arrest. When Christ was
   struck, the disciples scattered. When your Shepherd seems absent, you
   will be tempted to scatter. But hold on — the scattering is temporary.
   The Shepherd lives.

4. God refines through fire, not despite love but BECAUSE of it (v.9). The
   fire is not punishment — it is purification. Silver is refined to remove
   dross. Gold is tested to prove its purity. If you are in the fire, God is
   making you pure. The fire has a purpose and a limit.

5. "They shall say, The LORD is my God" (v.9). The goal of all refining is
   RELATIONSHIP. Not just theological knowledge but personal declaration:
   "MY God." Has your faith moved from "THE God" (abstract) to "MY God"
   (personal)? The fire makes faith personal.

14.notes:
Zechariah - Chapter 14
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The Day of the LORD culminates with all nations gathered against Jerusalem, the LORD standing on the Mount of Olives, living waters flowing from the city, and every survivor worshipping the King — "The LORD will be king over all the earth."

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Zechariah son of Berechiah. Prophesied 520-480 BC.
Contemporary with Haggai. Key theme: Messianic hope and the
coming kingdom. The most Messianic of the Minor Prophets.
Famous passages: the Branch (3:8, 6:12), "Not by might... but
by my spirit" (4:6), the triumphal entry (9:9), "they shall
look upon me whom they have pierced" (12:10), the shepherd
struck (13:7), and the LORD's return to the Mount of Olives
(14:4).

Classification: Prophetic Oracle — The Day of the LORD: The
Return to the Mount of Olives and Universal Kingdom
Key Themes: The final siege of Jerusalem, the LORD's physical
return to earth, the Mount of Olives split, living waters
flowing from Jerusalem, the LORD as King over all the earth,
universal holiness, the nations worshipping God

Historical Context: Zechariah 14 is the grand finale — not only
of this book but of the entire prophetic vision of the Old
Testament. It describes "the day of the LORD" in its ultimate
fulfillment: all nations gathered against Jerusalem, the city
taken, and then — the LORD Himself comes. His feet touch the
Mount of Olives (the same mountain from which Jesus ascended
in Acts 1:11-12). The mountain splits. Living waters flow.
And the LORD becomes "king over all the earth."

This chapter provides the most detailed OT description of
Christ's Second Coming and the Millennial Kingdom. The
geographical details (Mount of Olives, the valley, the living
waters) suggest literal fulfillment. The universality of
worship (all nations keeping the Feast of Tabernacles) points
to a time when all earthly authority submits to God's kingship.
The chapter ends with a remarkable image: even the bells on
horses and cooking pots in kitchens will be inscribed "HOLINESS
UNTO THE LORD." The sacred/secular divide is abolished — ALL
of life becomes holy.

Structure:
- The Nations Gathered Against Jerusalem (vv.1-2)
- The LORD Goes Forth to Fight (v.3)
- His Feet on the Mount of Olives (vv.4-5)
- The Unique Day: Neither Day Nor Night (vv.6-7)
- Living Waters from Jerusalem (v.8)
- The LORD King Over All the Earth (v.9)
- The Topographical Transformation (vv.10-11)
- The Plague on the Enemies (vv.12-15)
- The Nations Worship at the Feast of Tabernacles (vv.16-19)
- Universal Holiness: "HOLINESS UNTO THE LORD" (vv.20-21)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.4              | Acts        | 1       | 11-12              | "This Jesus... will come in the same way as you saw him go" (ESV) — from the Mount of Olives
v.4              | Revelation  | 19      | 11-16              | The return of Christ on a white horse, King of kings and Lord of lords
v.5              | 1 Thessalonians | 3       | 13                 | "The coming of our Lord Jesus with all his saints" (ESV)
v.5              | ?           | ?       | ?                  | Jude 14 — "The Lord comes with ten thousands of his holy ones" (ESV).
v.8              | Ezekiel     | 47      | 1-12               | Living waters flowing from the Temple, bringing life wherever they go
v.8              | Revelation  | 22      | 1-2                | The river of the water of life, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb
v.9              | Revelation  | 11      | 15                 | "The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ" (ESV)
v.9              | Deuteronomy | 6       | 4                  | "The LORD our God, the LORD is one" (ESV) — the Shema fully realized
v.20             | Exodus      | 28      | 36                 | "HOLY TO THE LORD" — originally on the high priest's forehead alone. Now on everything

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.4              | KJV         | "Mount of Olives" — Hebrew "Har haZetim" = the Mountain of Olives. East of Jerusalem, separated by the Kidron Valley. Jesus taught there, wept there, was arrested at its base (Gethsemane), and ascended from its summit. He will return to the same spot.
v.5              | KJV         | "saints" (KJV) / "holy ones" (ESV) — Hebrew "qedoshim" = holy ones. Could refer to angels, redeemed humans, or both. The NT suggests both: angels (Matt 25:31) and the redeemed (Rev 19:14, 1 Thess 3:13).
v.8              | KJV         | "living waters" — Hebrew "mayim chayyim" = flowing, running water (as opposed to stagnant cistern water). In John 7:38, Jesus applies this image to the Holy Spirit: "rivers of living water shall flow."
v.9              | KJV         | "one LORD, and his name one" — This echoes the Shema (Deut 6:4). In the kingdom, there will be no competing deities, no rival faiths, no polytheism. God alone will be acknowledged universally as the one true God.
v.20             | KJV         | "bells of the horses" — Hebrew "metsillot hassusim" = the metal pieces/bells on horse harnesses. The most ordinary, everyday object now bears the holiest inscription. This is the end of the sacred/secular divide.
v.21             | KJV         | "Canaanite" (KJV) / "trader" (ESV) — Hebrew "kena'ani" can mean either "Canaanite" (pagan inhabitant) or "trader/ merchant" (Canaanites were known as traders). The ESV reading suggests no more commercial exploitation in God's house — compare Jesus cleansing the Temple (Matt 21:12-13).

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "His feet shall stand on the Mount of Olives" (v.4). Jesus is coming
   back. Not metaphorically, not spiritually — His FEET will touch the same
   mountain from which He ascended. This is the Christian's ultimate hope:
   the physical, visible, bodily return of Jesus Christ to reign on earth.
   Live in light of His return.

2. "The LORD shall be king over all the earth" (v.9). One day, every rival
   authority will be dethroned. Every false god will be silenced. Every knee
   will bow. You are on the winning side. In a world of chaos and competing
   claims, remember: the outcome is settled. God wins. Completely.

3. "Living waters shall go out from Jerusalem" (v.8). From God's presence
   flows LIFE — continuously, in every season. In your summer and in your
   winter, the living water flows. Stay close to the source. Let God's river
   refresh you year-round.

4. "HOLINESS UNTO THE LORD" on the bells of horses (v.20). The end-goal of
   redemption is not that some things become holy while others remain
   secular — it is that EVERYTHING becomes holy. Your work, your cooking,
   your commute — all of it can bear the inscription "Holy to the Lord."
   Live ALL of life as worship.

5. The nations must CHOOSE to worship (vv.16-17). Even in the Millennial
   Kingdom, worship is a choice with consequences. Nations that refuse face
   drought. Today, the choice is the same: worship the King willingly and
   receive life — or refuse and experience barrenness. Choose today whom you
   will serve.
